calls.skip_reason: Post-booking kickoff call for an already-confirmed move — confirms existing booking details, reschedules date, and sends walkthrough link. No sales framework applies.
calls.top_strength: Handled a mid-call date change smoothly and proactively reserved two trucks to protect capacity.
calls.coaching_note: Skip — not a gradable sales call. Kickoff calls should still use the customer's name at least 3x as a rapport habit.

calls.phone_presence_notes: Polite and conversational but functionally transactional — acknowledged the movers' loading issue with rapport but never used the customer's name or built warmth intentionally.
calls.top_strength: Thorough probe — pulled storage unit number, access code, stair count, drive time context, and gave a well-reasoned 5-hour estimate with clear variables.
calls.coaching_note: Zero differentiators and no Save Your Ass speech are the critical gaps. This is a rebooking with an existing customer — perfect moment to reinforce No Worries Damage Coverage and On-Time Guarantee to build loyalty. Also use the customer's name at least 3 times.

calls.skip_reason: Post-booking kickoff call — customer already booked online, Donna is confirming details and adjusting the time estimate. No inbound lead sales framework applies.
calls.top_strength: Thorough item-by-item inventory walkthrough and good handling of a difficult, choppy connection without frustrating the customer.
calls.coaching_note: Skip — not a gradable sales call. Noteworthy: Donna never used the customer's name (Johnnie) once during a 13-minute call — this is a coaching opportunity for kickoff call quality.

calls.phone_presence_notes: Consistently upbeat and warm, offered multiple scheduling options with genuine flexibility, and closed the booking confidently without pressure.
calls.top_strength: Clean booking execution — confirmed two schedule options, gave a clear estimate range with scenario explanation, and handled the caller's scheduling uncertainty well.
calls.coaching_note: No differentiators landed at all — No Worries Damage Coverage, Meet Your Mover, On-Time Guarantee, and Save Your Ass were all missed. These need to be woven in before the estimate, not after booking. Customer name was never used (caller was a boyfriend, not the CRM contact — still, address by name when possible).

calls.skip_reason: Job interview / phone screen — not a customer sales call.
calls.top_strength: N/A — interview call.

calls.phone_presence_notes: Grant was consistently warm and professional, handled customer pushback on the estimate with patience rather than defensiveness, and the returning-customer context felt acknowledged and appreciated.
calls.top_strength: Thorough inventory intake — walked every room, garage, specialty items, and box count systematically; also handled a skeptical customer on estimate time professionally.
calls.coaching_note: Save Your Ass was completely missed — no consolidation speech, no carload tip, no mention that prep reduces the bill. Meet Your Mover and On-Time Guarantee were also absent. These three differentiators are table stakes on every full sales call. Also, the agenda opener was skipped — Grant jumped straight into availability rather than setting a brief roadmap for the call. Add those elements before moving to availability next time.

calls.phone_presence_notes: Calvin was warm, natural, and closed the call with a personal sign-off using Karen's name; the customer's tone was relaxed and responsive throughout.
calls.top_strength: Strong differentiator coverage — hit No Worries Damage Coverage, Meet Your Mover (movers on website with photos/bios), and On-Time Guarantee cleanly within a short call.
calls.coaching_note: Save Your Ass was fully missed — no consolidation speech, carload tip, or prep-reduces-bill language. The agenda opener was also skipped. Probe for info was light given the call was a follow-up (Cameron had already gathered the inventory), but Calvin could have confirmed key details rather than jumping straight to pricing. For a 7-minute call the differentiator coverage was solid; add SYA and you have a well-rounded short call.

calls.phone_presence_notes: Cameron was polite and thorough in data gathering but the interaction felt transactional — minimal warmth, no acknowledgment of the customer's situation (divorcing, downsizing), and the call ended with a basic handoff rather than a reassuring close.
calls.top_strength: Comprehensive profile build — captured all addresses including the storage stop, confirmed floor, elevator details, unit dimensions, and distance to apartment door with good thoroughness.
calls.coaching_note: As a walkthrough scheduler the primary job is a clean handoff — Cameron did that correctly. Opportunity areas: use the customer's name more than once, and bring a brief moment of human connection given she volunteered she was downsizing post-divorce. A single empathetic acknowledgment would meaningfully improve the experience.

calls.skip_reason: Internal/administrative call — Zachary was calling Verizon tech support to resolve an iPad configuration issue on a business account. No moving sales content.
calls.top_strength: N/A — not a sales call.
calls.coaching_note: N/A — not a sales call. Call should be filtered from grading queue.

calls.phone_presence_notes: Jespah's tone was genuinely warm, he offered unsolicited helpful advice on storage unit search (SpareFoot.com) and sizing, the customer responded positively and said 'I appreciate it' — rapport felt natural rather than scripted.
calls.top_strength: Exceptional customer service instinct — proactively helped Brian find a storage unit with a specific website recommendation and unit sizing guidance, going above the transaction to solve the customer's actual problem.
calls.coaching_note: Save Your Ass, Meet Your Mover, and On-Time Guarantee were all missed — the three key differentiators were not mentioned at any point. Clock mechanics (minimum, prorate) were vague and incomplete. Customer name was only used twice — one short of target. These are structural gaps. The natural rapport and helpful instincts are a real asset; now layer the full framework on top of that foundation. Also: note Brian — not Ryan — at the end of the call (rep called customer the wrong name).

calls.phone_presence_notes: Roselyn was polite and professional but the tone was transactional throughout — no warmth beyond baseline courtesy, and the call ended abruptly when the customer said he had to go.
calls.top_strength: Efficient triage — quickly identified the home exceeded the quote threshold, gathered the necessary details, and pivoted cleanly to scheduling a virtual walkthrough.
calls.coaching_note: Customer's name was never used during the call — a missed opportunity for connection on every exchange. The walkthrough scheduling offer was handled correctly but felt abrupt. When a customer says they have to go, offer to call or text them a scheduling link so the handoff doesn't fall through. Also: the online form submission check showed system lag — Roselyn handled it graciously, which was good.

calls.phone_presence_notes: Polite and professional with consistent 'yes sir' language, but purely transactional — no rapport-building or name use.
calls.top_strength: Clear and accurate pricing explanation — covered hourly rate, 2.5hr minimum, total estimate, and what the clock includes vs. excludes without being prompted.
calls.coaching_note: Zero on Set the Agenda and Save Your Ass are the biggest gaps. Dustin needs an opener that frames the call and builds value before pricing, and must add the consolidation/prep speech — especially on a minimal-item move where the customer is already questioning the minimum. No differentiators were mentioned at all (damage coverage, Meet Your Mover, On-Time Guarantee) — customer had competing quotes and got nothing to justify Einstein's price.

calls.skip_reason: Post-booking service call — customer calling to confirm existing move scheduled for tomorrow, not a new sales lead.
calls.top_strength: Strong damage coverage explanation and competitive positioning — Alex gave a thorough, unprompted breakdown of No Worries coverage vs. the industry 60-cent standard when the customer raised quality concerns.
calls.coaching_note: Call skipped from rubric scoring (post-booking service call). Noted for reference: Alex consistently mispronounced the customer's name as 'Judy' instead of 'Juhi' — a small but meaningful detail on a call where the customer was already anxious about quality.

calls.phone_presence_notes: Consistently warm and natural — used 'man' and 'of course man' in a way that felt genuine, and the customer responded openly and positively throughout.
calls.top_strength: Best clock section in the batch — explicitly covered 2.5hr minimum, 15-min prorate, on-site start with signature, and no warehouse-to-door charge, all in one clean pricing summary.
calls.coaching_note: No agenda opener and no real Save Your Ass are the two key misses. On a move this small (partial bedroom, ~$535–700), the consolidation speech is especially impactful — Landon had a natural opening when the customer mentioned taking boxes himself. Also needs to use the customer's name more than once; 'AJ' was said once at the top and never again.

calls.phone_presence_notes: Used 'Deb' and 'Deborah' warmly at the start, stayed patient and engaged while the customer rambled through a distracted inventory list, and the customer felt comfortable enough to be candid about sticker shock.
calls.top_strength: Strong situational flexibility — Joel quickly pivoted from a missed walkthrough to gathering enough inventory detail over the phone to rough-estimate the move and schedule a Saturday walkthrough to finalize.
calls.coaching_note: Walkthrough scheduler — no rubric score. For awareness: Joel's rough estimate range ($2,100–$3,000) was given without the standard differentiators or any Save Your Ass framing. When Debra reacted with sticker shock ('Jesus cri and chocolate pit'), Joel missed the opening to deliver the value pitch. Worth a quick coaching note for high-sticker-shock conversations.

calls.phone_presence_notes: Handled a long, rambling, ADHD-by-admission customer with patience and genuine warmth — never showed frustration, matched her energy well, and the customer ended the call laughing and confident.
calls.top_strength: Exceptional probing and booking close — Frank uncovered the full inventory across a chaotic 19-minute conversation (Murphy bed, workbench, extra appliances, bunk beds) and still secured the booking with a clear date, time, and realistic estimate range.
calls.coaching_note: Frank never used the customer's name once across a 19-minute call — zero is a meaningful miss on rapport. The bigger gap is no differentiators: no damage coverage, no Meet Your Mover, no On-Time Guarantee mentioned despite a large, expensive move with a customer who'd used Einstein before. The 'you're in good hands' line was the closest to a value pitch. Adding the No Worries speech and Meet Your Mover mention would take this call from good to great.

calls.top_strength: Thorough probing — confirmed exact floor distances, box count, and destination details before quoting.
calls.coaching_note: Deliver the full Save Your Ass prep speech on every call — it was completely absent and is the highest-value section.

calls.top_strength: Outstanding unprompted insurance explanation — delivered both No Worries and 60-cent baseline coverage.
calls.coaching_note: Save Your Ass prep speech was completely missing — adding it is the single fastest way to improve move efficiency.
calls.phone_presence_notes: Danah used Mia's name consistently, responded warmly to questions, and closed with genuine energy — customer felt comfortable throughout.

calls.top_strength: Exceptionally thorough probing — asked about floor, walk distances, elevator, unit number, room-by-room inventory.
calls.coaching_note: Save Your Ass was entirely absent — highest-leverage habit to build.

calls.top_strength: Clean efficient call structure — confirmed availability, built profile, gathered inventory, gave clear estimate in under 7 minutes.
